reason: Both runs render a sun with bloom, 6 labeled planets at varied distances, and readable labels. Three.js delivers a far stronger composition: an angled orbit-camera view with clear elliptical orbit rings, a glowing spherical sun, a ringed gas giant, and a rich starfield that frames the whole system as a recognizable solar system. Aura3D's view is nearly edge-on so the planets read as a flat horizontal row with no visible orbit paths, the sun renders as a malformed rounded-rectangle/capsule rather than a sphere, and its per-planet orbit multipliers are labeled in reverse (inner planets show larger 'x orbit' values). Aura3D satisfies the checklist but is visually weaker.
auraFailures: Sun renders as a rounded-rectangle/capsule shape instead of a glowing sphere
Near edge-on camera flattens the system into a horizontal row; no orbit rings/paths visible
Orbit-distance labels are inverted (inner Mercury '4.1x orbit' vs outer Nereid '0.7x orbit')
Camera does not convincingly frame an orbital system; reads as a lineup rather than orbits
threeFailures: Mercury label slightly overlaps the sun glow
